title: Weak first-order phase transition out of the classical kagome spin liquid
abstract: The low-temperature fate of the spin-liquid regime in the classical kagome Heisenberg antiferromagnet has been debated for over three decades. Using an expansion in the number of spin components, we show that, contrary to earlier Monte Carlo simulations, the spin liquid terminates at a weak first-order phase transition into the $\sqrt{3}\times\sqrt{3}$ phase which ordered moment saturates at zero temperature. Adding second-neighbor interactions, this transition belongs to a line of first-order phase transitions that ends at a critical point. For comparison, the pyrochlore antiferromagnet remains disordered at all temperatures.
summary: This paper resolves a long-standing debate by proving that the classical kagome spin liquid undergoes a weak first-order phase transition into an ordered phase. Using an advanced self-consistent theory, the authors demonstrate that the transition is a genuine thermodynamic event rather than a smooth crossover.
main_problem: Determining the low-temperature fate of the classical kagome Heisenberg antiferromagnet and resolving whether the transition from the spin liquid to the sqrt(3)xsqrt(3) phase is a thermal crossover or a thermodynamic phase transition.
main_result: The study shows that the spin liquid terminates at a weak first-order phase transition into the sqrt(3)xsqrt(3) phase, where the ordered moment saturates at zero temperature.
method: The authors use Nematic Bond Theory (NBT), an extension of the Self-Consistent Gaussian Approximation (SCGA) based on a large-Ns expansion, to solve for the free energy and identify phase stability.
model_or_system: A classical Heisenberg antiferromagnet on a kagome lattice with nearest-neighbor (J1) and potentially second-neighbor (J2) interactions.
key_observables: Free energy, ordered moment squared (m^2_AF), specific heat (cv), spin structure factor (S(q)), correlation length (xi), and latent heat (lh).
important_parameters: Temperature (T), second-neighbor coupling (J2), number of spin components (Ns), and system size (L).
main_assumptions: The NBT method neglects vertex corrections in the diagrammatic treatment and may overestimate critical temperatures by 10-20%.
figures_summary: Figures include specific heat vs. temperature for different system sizes, a J2-T phase diagram showing first-order transition lines and a triple point, real-space spin correlations, and finite-size scaling plots for latent heat and critical temperature.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of the kagome spin liquid fate, develops the NBT mathematical framework, presents the phase diagram and transition nature, compares results with Monte Carlo simulations, and discusses the extension to J2 interactions and the pyrochlore lattice.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
